title: Wagtail StreamField Quickstart for Technical Posts
excerpt: A DEV Wagtail article showing how structured content blocks can replace a plain text body.

value.title: Why StreamField fits a technical blog

body.value:
StreamField gives you structured authoring instead of a single giant text area. That is especially useful for technical writing where headings, code samples, diagrams, and callouts are part of the normal flow.

In this project, BlogPostPage.body already supports section headings, rich text, code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, and figures, so the seed data should mirror that model directly.

value.title: Implementation note
value.text: The Wagtail seeder should create BlogPostPage children under the /articles/ index page instead of inserting rows into the classic posts.Post table.

title: Designing Django Management Commands for Local DEV Reset
excerpt: A DEV Wagtail article that mirrors the reset-and-seed pattern used by the existing SQLite command.

body.value: The existing seed_dev_db.py command does four important things: it verifies DEBUG=True, confirms SQLite is used, deletes the database file, and runs migrations before seeding. A Wagtail-specific variant should keep that same pattern so local developer workflows stay predictable.

value.caption: Sequence used by the Wagtail seeder
value.code:
call_command("migrate")
call_command("bootstrap_wagtail_blog")
# create/update categories and tags
# create/update users
# create/update BlogPostPage pages under /articles/
# publish revisions

value.title: Important difference
value.text: Classic blog posts are ordinary Django model rows. Wagtail posts are pages in the page tree, so they must be added as children of the blog index page and published through revisions.

title: Using Mermaid Diagrams Inside Wagtail Blog Posts
excerpt: A DEV Wagtail article showing that the seeded body can include Mermaid blocks as first-class content.

body.value: The repo already includes a Mermaid block and a matching template, so the Wagtail DEV seed should include at least one example article that uses it. That makes the local CMS more representative and helps validate styling and rendering early.

value.caption: Wagtail seeding flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[seed_dev_wagtail_db] --> B[Delete SQLite DB]
    B --> C[Run migrations]
    C --> D[Create users]
    D --> E[bootstrap_wagtail_blog]
    E --> F[Create BlogPostPage pages]
    F --> G[Publish revisions]

value.title: Good DEV signal
value.text: If this page renders correctly, your StreamField JSON, templates, and front-end assets are all working together.
